Re: FPPC File No. A-90-309
Dear Ms. Stecher:
This }etter confi-rrns our telephone conversation on May 16,
1990, and requests written advice from the Commission pursuant to
covernment Code S83114 (b) .
I an requesting this advice on behalf of my cJ,ients:
Brad Gates, the incumbent Sheriff of orange county and a
candidate for re-el-ection to that office; Friends of Brad cates,
his contro]led campaign committee (the "Committeerr); and David R.
White II, CPA, the Conmittee's treasurer. I am authorized to
make this request on behalf of these clients.
on April, 20, 1990, f spoke lrith you by telephone concerning
the application of 585805(a) to the Committeers lease of its
office space. The relevant facts are as follows:
The Committee leases office space for its use, where it
keeps its records from past carnpaigns, its computer used for
comrnunications with supporters, a telephone and answering
machine, and other campaign-related material , and its furniture,
equipment and supplies, used in cates's campaigns and in his
incidental office holder activities. 
The Conmittee has a written
agreement for the l-ease of this office, for a term of one
calendar year, at the rate of $221.76 per month, for a total of
$2,661- 12 for the one year term.
The office is leased from OTRA, INC., a California
corporation in good standing, (The fease agreement is written
between the committee and ortega Equestrian center, which is a
fictitious 
business name of oTRA, INc., dul-y fited with the
orange county c1erk. ) oTRA, INc. is the master lessee of the
building in which the office is located, and the surrounding


JilI stecher, Esq.
May 17, 1990
Page 2
property. Title to the property is in the name of the lessor to
OTRA, INC. Neither the candidate, nor any member of his
immediate fanily, nor the Committeets treasurer, has any interest
in title 
to the property, or in any leasehold interest in the
property except as disclosed in this letter.
A11 of the shares of stock of OTRA, INC. are held in the
name of Diana L. cates, Brad catesrs wife. 
Brad cates hirnself ,
of course, has a community property interest in that stock.
The amount of the rent is computed on the basis of a rate of
9t.20 per square foot, per month. This rate was establ-ished by
OTRA, INC. as the fair rnarket value of the office space, based on
rental rates for other office space in the area.
$1,000 of the annual rent is forgiven by OTRA, INC., as a
non-nonetary contribution to the Connittee, which reports it as
such. The Committee pays OTRA, INC. the $l-,66]-.L2 balance of the
rent, for the lease of the office space.
In summary, the Connittee leases office space from a
corporation which is wholly owned by the wlfe of the candidate
who controls the Cornmittee.
fn this factual circumstarre, f asked you if, in the opinion
of the Comnission, S85805(a) prohibits the Comnittee from using
campaign funds to pay for the lease of this office space. You
responded to me by telephone on May l-6, and advised me that it is
the opinion of the Commissj,on that, for purposes of 585805(a), a
corporation is considered a IegaI entity separate from its
shareholders, and that the Conmitteets use of canpaign funds to
pay for lease of this office space is therefore not prohibited by
585805(a). f am requesting that you confirm this advice to me in
writing, pursuant to 583114 (b) . If you need any further
information before respondj.ng, please let me kno$r.
Thank you very much for your assistance in this rnatter.

Sacramento, CA 95804
Re: Interpretation of S85805 Discussed on Aprif 20
Dear Ms. Stecher:
on Friday, we discussed the interpretation of section
85805(a) of the Political- Reform Act, and whether the terminology
used in Chapter 7 of the Act, concerning conflicts of interest,
might apply to the interpretation of S85805(a). You indicated
that you would need to ana]yze this guestion further before
responding. I am sending you this letter to explain why I do not
bel-ieve that the rrconfl-ict of interestrt terminol-ogy was intended
by the Legislature to appfy to S85805(a), and why it does not.
Section 85805(a) prohibits reimbursement or payment of rent
where the lessee is, or 1egal title to the property resides in, a
candidate, an el-ected officer, or a menber of that personrs
inrnediate farnily, amonq others. The tenninology appears to be
carefully chosen so that the prohibition of that section wj.11
apply only to cases literall!, 
covered by it - that is, to
prohibit reirnbursement for rent pa j.d only where the lessee !g one
of the specified persons, and to prohibit payrnent of rent where
Ieqal- title 
resides ;!4 one of the speci.f j-ed persons.
Significantly, 
S858o5 does not use the terrninology of
chapter 7, which concerns a financial rrinterestfl of an individual
(S87100). The financia] 'interestu 
concerning real- property is
defined in S871-03(b) as a rrdirect or indirect interest" worth
$1,000 or more, and in 582033 as including real property owned
'rdirectly, indirectly, or beneficially,tr and incl"uding real
property rrof any business entityfl in which the individual owns,
directty, indirectly, or beneficially, a l-0? interest or greater.


JilI Stecher, Esq.
Aprj-I 25, 1990
Page Two
If the Legislature had wanted 585805(a) to have the same
broad scope as chapter 7, the Legislature could have prohibited
reimbursement or payrnent of rent for real property in which the
candidate or other person specified has an interest. 
The
definition of S82033 woufd then have been applicable to define
the type of ownership that srould be incl-uded in 585805(a).
The Legislature was presurnably aware of the broad definition
of 'rinterestrr in real property in 582033, however, when it
adopted S85805, but carefull-y used a different tennj.nology. This
different terminology has a narrovrer scope than does the
definition of "interest 
in 582033. As adopted by the
Legrislature, S85805(a) prohibits reimbursement or payment of rent
only where ttthe fC-E_Eeg i-g.t' or r'leqal- title 
resides . . . lprt one
of the persons specified. 
(Emphasis added) .
This terminoLogy is precisei its meaning is cl-ear and does
not need further definition for clarity; the definition in S82033
of rrinterest'r in real- property was presumabl-y not intended by the
Legislature to be applicable because different termj,nology is
used in 585805, and 582033 is not applicable because 585805 does
not use the tern rrinterest'r that is defined in 582033. For these
reasons, the coverage of S85805(a) shouLd be l-j.mited to the cases
that fal1 within its literal 
language.
We al-so discussed the interpretation of S85805, in Iight of
585301-, which limits the anount of canpaign contributions except
frorn a candidatets o$/n funds. If S85805(a) lvere to be
interpreted to prohibit payment of rent for property in which the
candidate has only an rrinteresttt as defined in 582033, where the
candidate does not have the rrlegal title'r Iitera11y required by
S85805(a), the following anomafous resufts $roufd occur where the
candidate's campaign connittee needs space with a fair market
value of more than $1,000 per year:
(1) The campaign comrnittee rroul-d be permitted to
have that campaign headquarters in property
leased or owned by the candidate in the
candidate's o$/n narne, because the candidate
could make a non-monetary contribution to the
campaign of the fuII fair market value;
(2) The carnpaign cornmittee woul-d be permitted to
have the carnpaign headquarters in property
l-eased or o$/ned by a corporation unrelated to
the candidate, because the carnpaign cou1d pay
the fuI] fair market value of the rent to
that corporation; but


Ji11 Stecher, Esq.
April. 25, L99 0
Page Three
(3) The canpaign conmittee would lqE be permitted
to have the campaign headquarters in property
leased or owned by a corporation in which the
candidate has an ownership interest, because
the corporation could not donate more than
$l-,000 of fair market value of rent to the
carnpaign, under 585301, but the canpaign
could not pay the corporation anything, under
that interpretation of 585805 (a) .
That pattern of results from such an interpretation of
585805(a) does not have any rational basis or purpose, and it is
not reasonable to think that the Legislature would have intended
it, where the literal 
l-anguage of S85805(a) does not require it.
Ms. stecher, I appreciate your taking the time to analyze
these Iegal issues. Please call me hrhen you are ready to discuss
this further. 
rf you would like any additional analysis from me,
please 1et me know.
ati4

Sincerely,

sections.question_synthetic: Whether California Political Reform Act Section 85805(a) prohibits a campaign committee from using campaign funds to pay rent for office space leased from a corporation wholly owned by the candidate's wife, given that the candidate has a community property interest in the corporation's stock.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter with an attached supplemental memorandum. No formal question/conclusion sections are present in a structured format. The attorney confirms a prior May 16, 1990 telephone conversation with Ms. Stecher where informal advice was given, and requests written confirmation. The April 25 letter provides detailed legislative history argument to support narrow interpretation of Section 85805(a).

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether California Political Reform Act Section 85805(a) prohibits a campaign committee from using campaign funds to pay rent for office space leased from a corporation wholly owned by the candidate's wife, given that the candidate has a community property interest in the corporation's stock.

embedding.summary: Attorney Darryl R. Wold requests written FPPC advice on behalf of Sheriff Brad Gates and his campaign committee regarding the application of Political Reform Act Section 85805(a) to the committee's lease of office space from a corporation owned by the candidate's wife. The attorney also provides a supplemental legal memorandum arguing that Section 85805(a) should be interpreted narrowly to apply only to literal ownership or legal title, not to indirect interests as defined in Chapter 7 of the Act.
